headline: Malaysia's six licensed exchanges list no dollar stablecoin. Malaysians hold them anyway.
context: The Securities Commission approves no dollar stablecoin on Malaysia's six licensed exchanges. USDT and USDC reach Malaysians off-platform. Bank Negara has published no count.

cards.big: Malaysia's six licensed exchanges approve no dollar stablecoin. The dollar arrived anyway, on rails Bank Negara has not publicly counted.
cards.sub: Licensed exchange volume hit RM13.9 billion in 2024, up from RM5.4 billion. None of that captures off-platform USDT and USDC.

cards.big: Standard Chartered modelled US$1 trillion in deposit flight from emerging-market banks into dollar stablecoins by 2028.
cards.sub: Egypt, Pakistan, Turkey, India and Brazil are among the high-risk markets named. Malaysia is not. The mechanism does not require naming.

cards.big: Tether has frozen US$4.4 billion in tokens globally, including US$2.1 billion at US-authority direction. The reach is extraterritorial.
cards.sub: The GENIUS Act, signed 18 July 2025, made on-chain sanctions compliance a federal obligation for permitted stablecoin issuers.

cards.big: Thailand approved USDT and USDC on its exchanges in March 2025. Singapore wrote rules for dollar stablecoins in 2023. Bank Negara's three 2026 pilots are all ringgit.
cards.sub: BNM promises clarity on ringgit stablecoins by end-2026. The pilots do not address the dollar demand.

cards.big: Malaysia walled off the ringgit in 1998. The dollar now arrives as software, and US sanctions decide who keeps it.
Like checking permits at the front door while every window is open. The permit list shows who applied, not who came in.
cards.sub: The SC approved list is the permit. P2P apps, offshore exchanges, and self-custody wallets are the windows.

cards.big: The dollar is now software. Six licensed exchanges do not list it. US sanctions can freeze it. Bank Negara has not published the count.
